lessons.title: Introduction to Large Language Models
lessons.concept_snapshot: Large Language Models (LLMs) are AI systems trained on vast amounts of text data to understand and generate human-like text. They operate using tokens, which are pieces of words or characters, and use context to predict the next token in a sequence. In FinTech, LLMs can analyze financial documents, generate reports, and assist in customer service by understanding complex queries. Understanding the fundamentals of LLMs involves grasping how they tokenize input, maintain context, and make predictions based on learned patterns.

lessons.title: Understanding Tokenization in LLMs
lessons.concept_snapshot: Tokenization is the process of converting text into smaller units called tokens, which are the basic building blocks for LLMs. In the context of FinTech, tokenization helps models understand and process financial documents, chat logs, or transaction data. Tokens can be words, characters, or subwords, depending on the language model's design. Effective tokenization is crucial for accurate predictions and context understanding in language models.

lessons.title: Context and Prediction in LLMs
lessons.concept_snapshot: Context in LLMs refers to the surrounding text that influences how the model predicts the next word or phrase. The model uses this context to generate coherent and relevant responses. In FinTech, understanding context is crucial for generating accurate financial insights and predictions. Effective use of context can enhance decision-making processes in trading platforms.

lessons.title: Understanding Tracing in AI Systems
lessons.concept_snapshot: Tracing in AI systems involves monitoring and logging the execution of AI models to understand their behavior and performance. It helps in identifying bottlenecks, debugging issues, and ensuring compliance with healthcare regulations. Key components include logging inputs, outputs, and intermediate states of models, as well as tracking data lineage. Effective tracing is crucial for maintaining transparency and accountability in AI-driven healthcare applications.

lessons.title: Introduction to Docker for AI Applications
lessons.concept_snapshot: Docker is a platform that enables you to package applications and their dependencies into a container, ensuring consistent environments across development, testing, and production. For AI applications in healthcare, Docker simplifies deployment and scaling by isolating the application from the host system. This isolation ensures that AI models can run reliably across different environments, which is crucial for maintaining compliance and performance in clinical settings. Docker also facilitates collaboration among AI/ML engineers by providing a standardized way to share and reproduce environments.

lessons.title: Introduction to AI Capabilities in Healthcare
lessons.concept_snapshot: AI in healthcare can process vast amounts of clinical data to assist in diagnostics, treatment planning, and operational efficiency. It excels in pattern recognition, such as identifying anomalies in medical images or predicting patient outcomes based on historical data. However, AI can also produce 'hallucinations'—incorrect or misleading outputs—due to biases in training data or misinterpretation of context. Understanding these capabilities and limitations is crucial for effective AI integration in healthcare settings.
